sections.title: MAS proposes AI risk management guidelines for financial institutions
sections.summary: MAS proposes Guidelines on Artificial Intelligence Risk Management to enhance oversight of AI risks in financial institutions. The Guidelines focus on AI risk management systems, policies, procedures, life cycle controls, and capabilities needed for AI use. They aim to establish generally applicable expectations across the financial sector that can be proportionally applied based on FI size and risk profile. The Guidelines will be updated as AI technology evolves.

requirements.text: MAS proposes to introduce Guidelines on Artificial Intelligence Risk Management
Guidelines focus on oversight of AI risk management in FIs, key AI risk management systems, policies and procedures, key AI life cycle controls, and capabilities needed for AI use
Guidelines aim to establish generally applicable expectations across the financial sector
Guidelines may be applied in a proportionate manner across FIs of different sizes and risk profiles
Guidelines should be generally applicable to different AI applications and technologies including Generative AI and AI agents
MAS will update or augment Guidelines when necessary due to evolving AI

sections.source_quote: MAS is proposing to introduce Guidelines on Artificial Intelligence (AI) Risk Management (the "Guidelines") 1 to enhance management of AI risks in financial institutions (FIs), and set out MAS' supervisory expectations relating to AI risk management in the financial sector.

sections.title: AI risks in financial sector including generative AI and AI agents
sections.summary: The section details risks associated with AI in financial institutions, particularly generative AI and AI agents. It highlights potential financial losses from poor AI model performance, operational disruptions, customer harm, security vulnerabilities, privacy risks, intellectual property issues, concentration risks, and human-factor risks. The section also references MAS's FEAT principles and initiatives like Veritas and Project MindForge to support responsible AI implementation.

requirements.text: Poor performance of AI models used for risk assessments could lead to substantial financial losses
Unexpected behaviours in AI systems could disrupt critical operations
Inappropriate outputs from customer-facing AI systems could result in harm or financial loss to customers
Generative AI introduces risks such as hallucinations, unpredictable behaviours, and challenges in explaining decision-making processes
Security vulnerabilities such as prompt injection attacks could occur
Privacy risks from potential data leakage when using third-party services could arise
Intellectual property violations from training on copyrighted content could occur
Concentration risks from over-reliance on a few dominant Generative AI providers could emerge
Human-factor risks arising from over-reliance on Generative AI could occur
AI agents with greater autonomy could introduce significant risks including misaligned actions and data exfiltration

sections.source_quote: The use of AI in the financial sector is not new. However, recent advancements in AI have led to an increased interest in leveraging AI in the financial sector.

sections.title: Applicability of AI risk management guidelines to financial institutions
sections.summary: The Guidelines apply to all financial institutions in Singapore. Branches or subsidiaries with parent entities in other jurisdictions may leverage their parent entities' AI risk management frameworks if they meet the Guidelines' expectations. FIs may implement the Guidelines proportionally based on their size, risk profile, AI usage, and specific use cases. The section defines AI scope to include models, systems, and use cases that learn from inputs to generate outputs, excluding predefined programming logic.

requirements.text: Guidelines apply to all FIs in Singapore
FI branches or subsidiaries with parent entities in other jurisdictions may leverage parent entities' AI risk management frameworks if they meet the Guidelines' expectations
FIs may implement Guidelines in a proportionate manner based on size, nature of activities, AI usage, and risk profiles
AI scope includes models, systems, and use cases that learn from inputs to generate outputs
Calculators or tools with outputs based solely on predefined programming logic are not considered AI under the Guidelines

sections.source_quote: MAS proposes to apply the Guidelines to all FIs.

sections.heading: AI Oversight
sections.title: AI Oversight Responsibilities for Financial Institutions
sections.summary: Financial institutions must establish robust frameworks, policies, and processes for identifying AI use cases, assessing risk materiality, maintaining AI inventories, and managing AI throughout its lifecycle. Board and senior management must foster an appropriate risk culture and ensure existing risk management practices are updated to address AI-related risks. If AI risk exposure is deemed material, FIs must create a dedicated cross-functional committee for oversight.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates specific governance structures for AI risk management, requiring institutions to identify AI usage, assess materiality, maintain inventories, and implement lifecycle controls. It directly impacts DTIAs by establishing baseline oversight requirements for cross-border AI deployments.

requirements.text: Board and senior management must establish frameworks, policies, and processes to identify AI use cases
Board and senior management must assess risk materiality of AI use cases
Board and senior management must maintain an up-to-date inventory of AI use cases
Board and senior management must manage AI throughout its lifecycle
Board and senior management must foster appropriate risk culture for AI
If AI risk exposure is material, establish a dedicated cross-functional committee
Update existing risk management practices to address AI-related risks

sections.source_quote: Board and senior management should also foster the appropriate risk culture for the use of AI, and ensure that existing organisation-wide risk management is updated to address risks introduced by the use of AI.

sections.heading: AI Oversight (cont.)
sections.title: AI Oversight Responsibilities
sections.summary: The Board and senior management must ensure consistent standards and coordination across financial institutions for AI risk management. They must establish clear roles, responsibilities, and policies for updating the Board on breaches of risk thresholds and AI incidents. Financial institutions should also form dedicated committees when AI risk exposure is material to ensure adequate oversight and address potential gaps in risk management.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates clear oversight responsibilities for AI risk management, requiring financial institutions to define roles, monitor risk thresholds, and establish reporting mechanisms for breaches. It directly impacts DTIAs by specifying governance structures and accountability frameworks for cross-border AI data transfers.

requirements.text: Monitor indicators and adherence to risk appetite thresholds
Articulate clear roles and responsibilities for managing AI risks across business lines and functions
Set out clear policies for updating the Board on breaches of risk appetite thresholds and AI incidents
Perform regular reviews to account for newer AI developments, changes in risk profile, business strategies, and regulatory developments
Ensure consistent standards, clear accountability, and robust coordination across the FI for AI risk management
Establish a dedicated cross-functional committee when overall AI risk exposure is deemed material
Ensure compliance with all existing regulatory requirements relevant to AI risk areas

sections.source_quote: The Board and senior management should ensure consistent standards, clear accountability, and robust coordination across the FI to manage AI risks.

sections.heading: AI Oversight (cont.)
sections.title: AI Lifecycle Controls and Data Management
sections.summary: Financial institutions must implement robust lifecycle controls for AI use cases, including identification, inventory, risk materiality assessment, and data management. They should define roles, conduct risk assessments, maintain accurate inventories, and ensure data quality, security, and privacy throughout the AI lifecycle. Third-party AI management requires specific onboarding and monitoring protocols.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes comprehensive lifecycle controls for AI, directly impacting DTIAs by requiring institutions to assess and manage data risks across borders, ensure data quality and privacy, and implement third-party AI oversight mechanisms.

requirements.text: Plan and implement robust controls covering the entire AI life cycle
Define use cases and assign roles for AI lifecycle across business units
Conduct risk materiality assessments and update inventory information
Implement lifecycle controls proportionate to assessed risk materiality
Develop contingency plans for high-risk AI use cases
Put in place data management controls for fit-for-purpose, representativeness, quality, classification, security, privacy, and auditability
Determine transparency and explainability requirements based on risk materiality
Define 'fair' outcomes and implement controls to mitigate harmful biases
Ensure appropriate human oversight across the AI lifecycle
Ensure adequate onboarding, development, and deployment controls for third-party AI

sections.source_quote: An FI should plan for and implement robust controls covering the entire life cycle of an AI use case, system or model.

sections.heading: AI Oversight (cont.)
sections.title: AI oversight requirements for third-party AI providers
sections.summary: Financial institutions must assess transparency, fairness, supply chain risks, concentration risks, and contingency plans for third-party AI providers. They must update legal agreements to clarify responsibilities, build staff capabilities, and conduct detailed assessments for complex AI products. FIs must ensure transparency and explainability where available, employ compensatory measures if not, and maintain robust oversight for high-risk AI deployments.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates specific oversight mechanisms for third-party AI providers, including transparency checks, supply chain assessments, and contingency planning. It directly impacts DTIAs by requiring FIs to evaluate third-party AI risks before integration and establish safeguards for high-risk deployments.

requirements.text: Assess transparency from third-party AI providers on how key risks are addressed during development and deployment
Set clear expectations on transparency needed from third-party AI providers
Consider compensatory measures if transparency and explainability are not available
Exercise due diligence regarding fairness practices of third-party AI providers
Check supply chain risk assessments for key third-party and open-source AI models
Assess potential concentration risks from over-reliance on key third-party AI providers
Develop robust contingency plans for third-party AI failures or discontinuation of support
Update legal agreements to clarify performance guarantees, data protection, audit rights, and notification clauses
Build staff awareness and capabilities for AI procurement, development, deployment and use
Conduct detailed assessments for complex third-party AI products with limited FI experience

sections.source_quote: An FI should consider the objectives and risks of the AI use case, system or model. The FI should require developers to justify and document their selection process, particularly when selecting more complex algorithms or less understood features over simpler or conventional alternatives.

sections.heading: AI Risk Management Capabilities
sections.title: AI Risk Management Capabilities for Financial Institutions
sections.summary: Financial institutions must ensure personnel involved in AI development and deployment possess adequate competence and conduct. This requires recruiting talent, providing training, and allocating resources proportional to the AI risk profile. Regular reviews are needed to maintain capabilities and address emerging AI technology risks.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes baseline capabilities for AI risk management that must be maintained across AI use cases, impacting how financial institutions assess and manage risks during data transfers involving AI systems.

requirements.text: Financial institutions should determine and ensure necessary competence and proper conduct of personnel involved in developing and deploying AI use cases, systems or models
Financial institutions should recruit necessary talent and provide appropriate training and support to equip employees with AI skills, knowledge and culture
Financial institutions should allocate adequate resources, including human, technological, and financial, proportionate to the AI risk profile
Financial institutions should conduct regular reviews to ensure personnel are equipped with adequate capabilities for effective AI risk management
Financial institutions should regularly review programmes for building capabilities and capacity to ensure they are up to date and incorporate training for newer AI technologies

sections.source_quote: An FI should determine and ensure the necessary competence and proper conduct of personnel involved in developing and deploying an AI use case, system or model. This includes recruiting the necessary talent and providing appropriate training and support to equip employees with the skills, knowledge, and culture for the appropriate use of AI, as well as effective AI risk management. Adequate resources, including human, technological, and financial, should be allocated proportionate to the risk profile of the AI use case, system or model.
